title: Reading your smart meter half-hourly data
subtitle: How to download and interpret HH consumption data to find your baseload and identify energy waste
excerpt: Every SMETS2 smart meter records electricity consumption in 30-minute intervals. That data is accessible through your supplier's app or website, or directly via the DCC (Data Communications Company) portal. Half-hourly data reveals your standby baseload, shows which appliances are running at night, and lets you set an accurate time-of-use tariff schedule.
sourceNotes: gov.uk Smart Meter Implementation Programme (SMIP) guidance. Ofgem smart meter rollout documentation. DCC (Data Communications Company) consumer data access guide.

glossaryTerms.definition: Kilowatt-hour. The unit of energy used on electricity and gas bills. 1 kWh equals the energy consumed by a 1,000 W appliance running for one hour.

content.text: The average UK household uses 3,200 to 4,500

of electricity per year. Around 400 to 800 kWh of that is standby power: appliances left on overnight in sleep or idle mode. Half-hourly data shows you exactly which 30-minute periods are high and low, letting you pinpoint the source of unexpected consumption without buying a separate energy monitor.
How to access your half-hourly data

If you have a SMETS2 smart meter (installed after 2018 in most cases), your data is available in three ways. First, your energy supplier's app typically shows a daily bar chart; some suppliers also offer a half-hourly view within the app. Second, the Bright, Hildebrand, or n3rgy apps connect directly to the DCC and pull your half-hourly data with your permission, showing a 48-column daily chart going back 13 months. Third, you can download a CSV of your half-hourly data from your supplier account and open it in any spreadsheet.

Look at consumption between midnight and 06:00. In a house with only standby devices running, this should be 0.1 to 0.3 kWh per half-hour. If you see 0.5 kWh or more consistently overnight, something is drawing 1,000 W or more at night. Common culprits are: an old chest freezer (150 to 300 W), a fish tank heater and pump (100 to 200 W), an electric oil-filled radiator left on low, or a pool pump (if applicable). A single 15-year-old upright freezer running continuously uses 600 to 800 kWh per year at 2025/26 rates, costing 162 to 216 pounds: replacing it with an A-rated model saves 200 to 400 kWh per year.

Once you know your half-hourly consumption shape, you can assess whether an Agile, Go, or other time-of-use tariff suits your household. If your overnight baseload is below 0.1 kWh per half-hour and you can shift dishwasher and washing machine runs to off-peak periods, the saving is typically 40 to 100 pounds per year. Off-peak periods are typically 00:30 to 04:30 on Economy 7, or the cheapest slots on Agile, at 10 to 12p/kWh versus peak rates of 35 to 40p/kWh. The half-hourly data gives you the actual volume to shift, so the calculation is specific rather than generic.

SMETS1 meters (installed before 2018) may not have enabled half-hourly data sharing. Contact your supplier; most SMETS1 meters have been remotely enrolled onto the DCC network since 2022 and should now be accessible. If you have a gas smart meter, half-hourly gas data is also available and useful for tracking boiler cycling and hot water use. The same analysis applies: overnight gas consumption on a non-heating-season night reveals whether the hot water cylinder is losing more than expected.

attrs.symptom: Supplier app shows only daily totals, not half-hourly

content.text: Not all supplier apps expose the half-hourly view. Use the Bright or Hildebrand app instead: they connect to the DCC directly with your permission.

attrs.symptom: Data missing for periods or showing zero

content.text: Smart meter communication loss is common in areas with poor cellular signal. The meter records locally and uploads in batches; gaps usually fill in within 24 to 48 hours.
